--- SOURCE 2: State of Washington Creative Economy Working Definition ---
URL: http://choosewashingtonstate.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/04/Creative-Economy-Definition-Final-7-15-22.pdf

SUMMARY:
Howkins defined the creative economy as “ the transactions of creative products that have an economic good or service that results from creativity and has economic value .”1 He further defined the key activities of the creative economy as advertising, architecture, art, crafts, design, fashion, film, music, performing

FULL CONTENT:
State of Washington Creative Economy Working Deﬁnition July 15, 2022 State of Washington Creative Economy Strategic Plan Project Team: 2 Table of Contents INTRODUCTION 3 DEFINING THE CREATIVE ECONOMY 4 Measuring the Creative Economy: United States 8 Core Domains 10 Applied Arts and Design Services Domains 10 Transversal Domains 10 MEASURING THE CREATIVE ECONOMY: CANADA 11 WASHINGTON STATE’S CREATIVE’S ECONOMY 13 INTRODUCTION 13 STATE OF WASHINGTON OVERVIEW 14 STATE OF WASHINGTON REGIONS 19 CASCADES TO COAST CREATIVE ECONOMY SUMMARY 22 MID SOUND CREATIVE ECONOMY SUMMARY 26 NORTH CENTRAL CREATIVE ECONOMY SUMMARY 30 NORTHEAST CREATIVE ECONOMY SUMMARY 34 NORTHWEST CREATIVE ECONOMY SUMMARY 38 SOUTH CENTRAL CREATIVE ECONOMY SUMMARY 42 SOUTH SOUND CREATIVE ECONOMY SUMMARY 47 SOUTHEAST CREATIVE ECONOMY SUMMARY 51 SOUTHWEST CREATIVE ECONOMY SUMMARY 56 METHODOLOGIES 60 WORKING CREATIVE ECONOMY DEFINITION 60 3 Introduction The intent of this document is to provide a baseline definition of the creative economy for the State of Washington Creative Economy Strategic Plan. This definition will evolve throughout the planning process, as more quantitative data and qualitative stakeholder discussions inform the state’s definition and the resulting plan. Since there are diverse definitions of the creative economy across sectors, a summary of the various approaches used internationally, nationally, and locally are provided as a methodological and contextual base for the proposed State of Washington working definition presented. National and international creative economy measurement protocols (such as North American Industry Classification System [NAICS] and Security Operation Center [SOC] codes) are also briefly reviewed, including the current frameworks used in Washington State to define the creative economy. Using Washington’s Department of Commerce current code grouping of creative economy sectors, the standard definition used in Western States Arts Federation’s (WESTAF’s) Creative Vitality Suite, the industry standard data solution for measuring the for-profit and nonprofit arts, culture, and creative sectors, and the Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development’s (OECD’s) creative economy framework, a profile of the State’s creative industries and occupations provides the baseline definition for consideration. Western States Arts Federation’s Creative Vitality Suite aggregates multiple streams of data to provide the most complete picture of the creative economy available. These streams include data from partners and vetted and trusted government institutions such as Lightcast (formerly Emsi), the Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS), the Bureau of Economic Analysis (BEA), the Internal Revenue Service (IRS), and the National Center for Charitable Statistics (NCCS). At this phase of the project lifecycle, the research team has reviewed a wide range of descriptive (or conceptual) definitions but has focused on building a data-based definition to establish a methodological grounding for the project. This working definition includes both industry and occupational code groupings drawn from several data sources including Lightcast and CVSuite datasets used by the Department of Commerce and ArtsWA, respectively. This definition can be found in the Methodologies section. A descriptive definition will be derived from further examination of the literature, consultation with stakeholders across the state, and deliberations with the Department of Commerce and authorizers. The objective of generating a definition in the context of the strategic plan is to build on and deepen the work key stakeholders have already done in defining the creative economy of the state of Washington. 4 Defining The Creative Economy Defining the creative economy for a country, region, or community is a challenging task, as there is not one universally accepted definition. Each region/area determines what is relevant to their creative economy definition, based on a set of industries that encompass culture, art, media, technology, and innovation sectors. Previous work in the field has produced two generally accepted ways of describing…" or: Previous work in the field has established two generally accepted ways of describing the creative economy— a descriptive definition and a definition based on datasets. These definitions are used together and inform one another in order to provide a more comprehensive understanding of a creative economy. The descriptive definition typically consists of conceptual groupings of similar types of economic activity in order to create a general framework. The data-based definition typically consists of a list of industries and occupation codes, such as NAICS and SOC codes. Data-based definitions are most often derived from a larger conceptual model of the creative economy. Economists have attempted defining the creative economy at several geographical scales, from local to global. One of the first globally accepted baseline definitions was based on work by John Howkins et al. in 2002. Howkins defined the creative economy as “the transactions of creative products that have an economic good or service that results from creativity and has economic value.”1 He further defined the key activities of the creative economy as advertising, architecture, art, crafts, design, fashion, film, music, performing arts, publishing, research and development, software, toys and games, television and radio, and video games.2 The research team considers that these industries, at a minimum, would form part of a working definition of the creative economy for the purposes of developing a creative economy strategic plan. The creative economy is the subject of significant academic research but has equally become a global agenda taken up by both the public sector and private enterprise. In Economics and Culture (2000), David Throsby views the creative economy as an area of both cultural and political debate, stating the "two demarcation lines concerning this issue can be identified: the first is established by academic discourse, and the second is set by policy discourse. The cultural approach connected with the 1 Howkins, John, 2002. Creative Economy: How People Make Money from Ideas.” London, Allen Lane. pp. 88-117. 2 Howkins, John, 2002. Creative Economy: How People Make Money from Ideas.” London, Allen Lane. 5 concept of cultural and creative industries is pursued within cultural studies, sociology, and political economy, while the industry aspect is pursued within applied economics and practical policy studies.”3 These diverse points of view give rise to differences in terminology and to the approach to defining the creative economy. A working group of the National Creativity Network, the Creative Economy Coalition (CEC) studied 27 different definitions of the creative economy in 2013 and found creative industries are under-recognized and undercounted. The Creative Economy Coalition’s study examined 25 reports by regional, state, and local arts organizations that use NAICS codes to define the creative economy. The CEC found a total of 264 NAICS codes named across the 25 reports: four codes were present in all 25 reports, and 70 codes were common to 50% or more of the reports.4, 5 Specifically, the study determined (1) there is significant undercounting of the creative sector since government data cannot provide detailed self-employment data or identify emerging new creative businesses; (2) NAICS and SOC codes vary state to state in terms of accuracy; (3) there is a distinct lack of recognition of the creative industry sector as an economic contributor; and (4) the current perception is that the creative economy is not an export industry. According to the more recent Creative Industries 4.0 Report released in April 2022 by the UN Conference on Trade and Development (UNCTAD), the Creative Economy, sometimes referred to as the Orange Economy, is defined “with the perspective or chosen objective of the observer”6— aligning with the thoughts of varied definitions across the globe. The Creative Industries Report assesses and synthesizes many of the global creative economy definitions and groups them into the following categories. 1. Economic Value: While there are many authored opinions about the economic value of creativity, it is widely agreed that creativity becomes an economic activity when it produces an idea with economic implications or becomes a tradeable 3 Throsby, David, 2000. Economics and Culture, Cambridge Books, Cambridge University Press. 4 Creative Economy Coalition, America’s Creative Economy: A Study of Recent Conceptions, Definitions, and Approaches to Measurement Across the USA, August 2013, p62, https://www.arts.gov/sites/default/files/Research-Art-Works-Milwaukee.pdf. 5 6UN Conference on Trade and Development (UNCTAD) Creative Industries Report 4.0, April 2022. https://unctad.org/topic/trade-analysis/creative-economy-programme 6 product, most often—but not exclusively—through intellectual property rights. A number of approaches, including the definition advanced by the Department of Culture, Media and Sport of the United Kingdom (DCMS), recognize creativity’s contribution to jobs and wider economic activity. The DCMS’s influential early definition of the creative industries describes them as “those industries which have their origin in individual creativity, skill and talent and which have a potential for wealth and job creation through the generation and exploitation of intellectual property.” Since the creative economy has been explored as an economic development option, particularly by large cities, the socioeconomic value of the sectors has become increasingly defined not only in the production and consumption of creative products and symbols but also in terms of broader impact on economic development. 2. Cultural and Sociological Value: Cultural and sociological approaches to defining the creative industries tend to focus on cultural, symbolic, social, and philosophical—even existential—value. The United Nations Education Science and Culture Organization (UNESCO), while following the same line of thought, focuses more explicitly on cultural value and creative content, defining the cultural and creative industries as “those sectors of organized activity that have as their main objective the production or reproduction, the promotion, distribution or commercialization of goods, services and activities of content derived from cultural, artistic or heritage origins.”7 One of the earlier definitions, introduced by Markusen, Wassall, DeNatale and Cohen (2008),8 restricts the definition to activities directly involved in the production of social meaning in the form of texts and symbols. This definition includes television, radio, the cinema, newspapers, magazine and book publishing, music recording and publishing industries, advertising, and the performing arts. “Arts of living, doing, and being, not just arts: that’s what culture is about” is offered by Mercer,9 who views the creative economy more through an anthropological and sociological lens. 7 https://en.unesco.org/creativity/sites/creativity/files/digital-library/What%20Do%20We%20Mean%20by%20CCI.PDF 8Markusen, A., G. Wassall, D.DeNatale and R.Cohen (2008) ‘Defining the Creative Economy: Industry and Occupational Approaches,’ Economic Development Quarterly, Vol. 22 No. 1, February pp.24-45. 9Mercer, C. (2009) ‘Convergence, Creative Industries and Civil Society’ Culture Unbound, 1, 179–204. Hosted by Linköping University Electronic Press. 7 3. Business Value: The business value perspective views the creative economy from the vantage point of product development, innovation, and entrepreneurship. Innovation is part of product development, including idea generation, idea screening, concept testing, and development. The InterAmerican Development Bank (IADB) proposes a definition of the creative economy (orange economy) with the following elements: (1) creativity, arts and culture as productive endeavors; (2) products strongly related to intellectual property rights (primarily copyrights); and (3) activities with a direct role in the value chain transforming ideas into products.10 An integrated definition offered by UNCTAD, and the United Nations Development Programme (UNDP) encompasses artistic and cultural, economic, and business aspects, stating: ● The Creative Economy is an evolving concept based on creative assets potentially generating economic growth and development; ● It can foster income generation, job creation and export earnings while promoting social inclusion, cultural diversity, and human development; ● It embraces economic, cultural, and social aspects interacting with technology, intellectual property, and tourism objectives; ● It is a set of knowledge-based economic activities with a development dimension and cross-cutting linkages at macro and micro levels to the overall economy; and ● It is a feasible development option calling for innovative multidisciplinary policy responses. The recognition of the importance of the creative economy for overall economic performance is growing, which implies the importance for policymaking will continue to increase.11 Therefore, the positive socioeconomic impacts of a creative economy are also likely to increase in value for communities as policies are advanced to support this key industry sector. A 10UN Conference on Trade and Development (UNCTAD) Creative Industries Report 4.0, April 2022. https://unctad.org/topic/trade-analysis/creative-economy-programme. 11 Deloitte (2021). ‘The Future of the Creative Economy,” Deloitte LLP, United Kingdom. 8 comprehensive review of publications analyzing creative economy impacts by Daubaraite and Startiene (2015) finds that creative industries influence the national economy through:12 ● Fighting unemployment, in particular youth unemployment; ● Contributing to gross domestic product (GDP); ● Contributing to exports; ● Contributing to social inclusion; ● Contributing to social and cultural development; and ● Increasing the quality of life. Measuring the Creative Economy: United States Tasked to define and capture the economic value of the arts and cultural sector, the Bureau of Economic Analysis (BEA) developed the most accurate definition of the creative economy in the United States. The definition was framed using a macroeconomic approach called satellite accounting. Satellite accounts are meant to serve as complementary accounts to a country’s system of national income and product accounting. Satellite accounts allow for additional analysis on a specific sector by linking supplementary information (such as data collected by private industry) to existing national accounts and standards. This permits economists to study these sectors without overburdening or disrupting the central system. This type of economic analysis has been used in fields that have cross-sector activities such as tourism, transportation and, notably, culture. The U.S. and Canada both use cultural satellite accounts to measure the economic impact of arts and culture. Each of these countries, and many others that use this approach, have slight variations on definitions and scope of culture when it comes to measurement; however, they both use the same method. These definitions can also change over time based on evolution of the industries and their relationships. 12Daubaraite, U. and G. Startiene (2015). ‘Creative industries impact on national economy in regard to sub-sectors,’ Procedia-Social and Behavioral Sciences, 213 pp.129–134. 9 In 2013, the National Endowment for the Arts (NEA) worked with the U.S. Department of Commerce’s Bureau of Economic Analysis (BEA) to establish a new system for valuing arts and culture in the U.S. called the Arts and Cultural Production Satellite Account (ACPSA).13 The goal of the ACPSA is to measure the industry output (the market value of goods and services produced by an industry) and the value added (the gross domestic product by industry). The ACPSA also estimates arts and cultural employment and compensation. To accomplish this, the BEA uses several recognized data sources to create the ACPSA. The BEA also uses classification codes from the NAICS to group business establishments into industries according to similarity in the process used to produce goods and services.14 To select the commodities and industries that would be included in the ACPSA, a framework was developed which divided arts and cultural industries into three domains: core, applied arts and design services, and transversal.15 Each domain also has subdomains. When referring to the definition of culture for the satellite account, the BEA says, “Culture can be defined in a variety of ways, including language, traditions, beliefs, and values. For this account, arts and cultural production is defined narrowly to include creative artistic activity—the goods and services produced by it, the goods and services produced in support of it, and the construction of buildings in which it takes place.”16 13Iyengar, Sunil, and Bonnie Nichols. NEA Guide to the U.S. Arts and Cultural Production Satellite Account. National Endowment for the Arts, 2013, https://www.arts.gov/sites/default/files/nea_guide_white_paper.pdf. 14 Attach .xlsx with full list of NAICS codes used for ACPSA. 15 Transversal domains are domains that are found across fields, such as education, training, administration, funding, and government. 16 https://www.bea.gov/news/2022/arts-and-cultural-production-satellite-account-us-and-states-2020 10 Core Domains Museums, libraries, and culture centers Live Performance and music Visual arts Written works Audio-visual and interactive media Applied Arts and Design Services Domains Transversal Domains Advertising Other design services Education Governance, funding, and professional design services Infrastructure Through a partnership with the National Assembly of State Arts Agencies, the ACPSA data is disaggregated into state-level creative economy reports that provide a highly credible source of information to state arts agencies and state level advocates across the nation. In 2020, for example, the Creative Economy State Profile for Washington, as reported by the National Assembly of State Art Agencies and the U.S. Bureau of Economic Analysis, disclosed that arts and cultural production accounts for $62,392,906,000 and 10.3% of the Washington economy, contributing 180,262 jobs. The top five industries by value added using location quotients are publishing, information services, grantmaking and giving, and construction. In addition to data from the satellite account, the majority of states and jurisdictions in the U.S. have published state-wide reports on the impact of the creative economy. Based on the 2013 CEC analysis on the conceptions and definitions of the creative economy across the U.S., state-level reports use either the NAICS, SOC, or a combination of the two codes when identifying what industries and occupations should be included in the definition of the creative economy of their state. 11 17 According to the National Assembly of State Arts Agencies Interactive Database of State Creative Economy Studies, 141 state-level creative economy studies have been conducted across 48 U.S. states and jurisdictions since 2001, reflecting a wide range of approaches to defining the creative economy.18 In conducting further analysis over the course of the project, researchers will compare and contrast findings with the ACPSA and examine the creative economy studies of states that have been selected for comparison. Measuring the Creative Economy: Canada Canada’s national standard of measuring the economic importance of arts and culture is methodologically like that of the United States. The Canadian Cultural Satellite Account (CSA) is sponsored by the federal Department of Canadian Heritage and its various partners and is developed and maintained by Statistics Canada. The key difference between the two countries is that the Canadian CSA also includes sport. However, the data and impact of sport is disaggregated as a subsection of the account. For the CSA, culture is defined as “creative, artistic activity and the goods and services produced by it, and the preservation of heritage.” Sport is defined as “an individual or group activity, often pursued for fitness in leisure time, fun or competition.”19 One major difference 17 Creative Economy Coalition, America’s Creative Economy: A Study of Recent Conceptions, Definitions, and Approaches to Measurement Across the USA, August 2013, p26, https://www.arts.gov/sites/default/files/Research-Art-Works-Milwaukee.pdf. 18 https://nasaa-arts.org/research/interactive-database-state-creative-economy-studies/. 19 https://www150.statcan.gc.ca/n1/pub/13-605-x/2020001/article/00002-eng.htm. 12 between the U.S. and Canada's definitions of culture is that Canada includes "the preservation of heritage." Like the U.S. ACPSA, the CSA selects NAICS codes that pertain to culture and sport and reorganizes them in a coherent manner based on the Canadian Framework for Culture Statistics.20 This framework was developed and agreed upon in 2011 by a group of cultural organizations across Canada.21 It is very similar to the framework used by the NEA but separates sound recording into its own domain. Cities and regions within Canada have been selected for comparison, so an understanding of the Canadian approach to defining the creative economy will be important to the process. 20 Attach .pdf with full list of NAICS codes used for CSA, https://www150.statcan.gc.ca/n1/pub/13-607-x/2016001/1266-eng.htm. 21 “Culture Domains” screen capture from: https://www.canada.ca/en/canadian-heritage/corporate/publications/general-publications/culture-satellite-account/video-updating-csa.html. 13 Washington State’s Creative’s Economy Introduction As previously noted, the working definition being proposed here is a baseline for the planning process. The specific code groupings within the definition are outlined in the Methodologies section. The data analysis that follows utilizes the working definition to present initial statistics on the creative economy of the State and its regions. The Washington State Department of Commerce segments Washington into 9 different commerce regions: 1) Northwest 2) Mid Sound 3) Cascades to Coast 4) South Sound, 5) Southwest, 6) North Central 7) South Central 8) Northeast and 9) Southeast. Using Washington’s Department of Commerce currently defined creative economy sectors, the CVSuite standard definition of the creative economy, and the Creative Vitality Index, a profile is presented of the State’s creative industries and occupations.22 Historically, Washington’s economy is strong and contributes significantly to the real gross domestic product (GDP) of the country, most recently contributing 8.3% to the total U.S. GDP in 2021 Q4. Although all states are feeling the economic impacts of COVID, the GDP decreased in 46 states and the District of Columbia in the first quarter of 2022, and real GDP for the nation decreased at an annual rate of 1.6 percent.23 Consistent with these trends, Washington State’s contributions to the national GDP decreased by 3.3% in the first quarter of 2022. Pre-COVID, however, the Washington economy expanded almost 40% in 2018 and 2019 (according to the Washington State Employment Security Department data) and outperformed almost every 22 !"#$%&#'()*#$+)(',)(-.$/01#2$3456'&#7$("#$6#&$3'6)('$3403#0(&'()40$48$3&#'()*#$'3()*)(-$)0$(94$&#:)407;$<'('$40$3&#'()*#$)01=7(&)#7>$ 433=6'()407>$'01$3=,(=&',$0406&48)($&#*#0=#7$'&#$)01#2#1$=7)0:$'$646=,'()40?@'7#1$3',3=,'()40;$!"#$&#7=,()0:$%+/$+',=#$7"497$'$&#:)40A7$ 3&#'()*#$*)(',)(-$3456'&#1$(4$'04("#&$&#:)40;$!"#$%+/$)7$@=),($8&45 three indicators: number of jobs within specific creative occupations, industry sales from specific industries, and arts and culture nonprofit revenues. These indicators give a broad picture of the health of a region’s overall creative economic health. Choosing the exact indicators stemmed from conversations around combining economic development and nonprofit sector methodologies to help the field better understand the creative economy. Once thought to be independent areas of study, the for-profit and nonprofit indicators were chosen due to their correlation with a region’s overall creative economic health. 23 U.S. Bureau of Economic Analysis (BEA). Gross Domestic Product by State, 1st Quarter 2022. 14 state in the nation. The Seattle metropolitan area, which is home to just over half of the state’s population and employment, accounts for the bulk of this economic activity.24 State of Washington Overview $60 Billion Industry Earnings ⬆ 17% since 2019 202,290 Occupation Jobs ⬇ 6% since 2019 1.00 CVI Value ⬇ 1% since 2019 This project primarily analyzes the creative economy by its creative industries and creative workers, and, according to CVSuite data, Washington’s creative industries generated $59 billion in earnings in 2020, 16% of all dollars earned in the state. Within the state, the creative industries support 351,184 jobs statewide. Washington’s high average earnings of $170,107 per job, compared to the national average of $89,542, makes the state extremely attractive for creative businesses and talent. The top industries that make Washington’s creative economy unique, as measured by location quotient,25 include software publishers, electronic shopping and mail-order houses, internet publishing and broadcasting and web search portals, wineries, and musical instrument manufacturing. The independent artists, writers, and performers industry has a high location quotient of 1.14 and employs 29,830 jobs in the state, the fourth largest industry measured by jobs. When measuring only creative workers, the state employs 202,290 full-time, part-time, self-employed, and gig economy jobs. The largest occupation groups that employ the most creative workers are photographers (23,293 jobs), bartenders (12,820 jobs), marketing managers (12,406 jobs), writers and authors (11,459 jobs), and graphic designers (10,211 jobs). Musicians and singers (10,095 jobs) and fine artists, including painters, sculptors, and illustrators (6,771 jobs) are in the top 10 occupations within the state. When looking at the workforce through location quotient, the data suggests that these occupations have a higher concentration of jobs: entertainment attendants and related workers; architects; marketing managers; furnace, kiln, oven, drier, and kettle operators and tenders; special effects artists; and animators. 24 2019 Labor Market Economic Report.pdf. Washington State Employment Security Department. January 2020. 25 Location quotient quantifies the concentration of an industry or occupation against the nation to reveal the uniqueness within a region. 15 Washington has 9 distinct commerce regions, each with their own economic specialties. A common theme across the commerce regions was a decline in jobs from 2019 to 2020 but an increase in average earnings per job during the pandemic recession. The increase in earnings could be attributed to the stimulus funds added to the economy or a combination of the resiliency of the creative industries. Future analysis of trends across the Commerce regions with 2021 data will deepen our understanding of the impact the pandemic had on the creative sector. The pandemic opened a new way of acquiring talent through remote channels, causing (forcing?) businesses to offer greater compensation (and incentives) to attract competitive talent. For example, North Central showed a 17% increase in average annual earnings per creative worker. Ninety-three percent of creative industries’ activity (in terms of industry earnings) comes from the Mid Sound commerce region consisting of King County. Mid Sound contains a high density of technology jobs in industries such as software publishing, electronic shopping and mail-order houses, and internet publishing and broadcasting and web search portals. The other 8 commerce regions have relatively smaller creative economies. When looking at creative industries by location quotient within the commerce regions, Cascades to Coast has a high concentration of musical instrument manufacturing; books printing; and sewing, needlework, and piece goods stores jobs. North Central has an exceptional concentration of winery jobs compared to the other regions. As demonstrated through the data, each Washington region tells its own unique economic story, yet Washington remains one of the strongest creative economies in the nation. 16 State of Washington Creative Industries These charts present data on the industries and occupations that make up the Washington creative economy. Taken together, this data shows the importance of technology to Washington’s creative economy in terms of industry segments and the significance of artists and designers among creative occupations. --- SOURCE 4: Creative Economy Outlook 2024 - UNCTAD ---
URL: https://unctad.org/publication/creative-economy-outlook-2024

SUMMARY:
# Creative Economy Outlook 2024 UNCTAD’s global survey reveals the varied economic contributions of the creative economy across different countries, ranging from 0.5% to 7.3% of GDP and employing between 0.5% to 12.5% of the workforce. Digitalization is transforming the creative economy. Market concentration poses significant challenges to the creative economy. Inclusion and environmental sustainability are becoming a cornerstone of the creative economy. #### How digitalization is transforming the creative economy Chapter I: Global trends in the creative economy Chapter III: Digitalization and artificial intelligence in the creative economy Chapter IV: Market concentration and competition in the creative economy * Trade and the creative economy * Creative Economy Report (Series)") * Creative Economy and Industries Creative economy

# Creative Economy Outlook 2024

* [Highlight](#tab-1)
* [Downloads](#tab-2)



The Creative Economy Outlook 2024 underscores the pivotal role that creative industries play in trade and economic growth. UNCTAD’s global survey reveals the varied economic contributions of the creative economy across different countries, ranging from 0.5% to 7.3% of GDP and employing between 0.5% to 12.5% of the workforce.

*The creative economy has the right forces pushing its sails. This is not just art. It is an economic powerhouse that we must harness together, leaving no one behind.*

**Rebeca Grynspan**

Secretary-General of UN Trade and Development (UNCTAD)

### Growth was fastest in creative services

Creative services exports surged to $1.4 trillion in 2022, marking a 29% increase since 2017. Meanwhile, creative goods exports reached $713 billion, a 19% increase. Over the past decade, creative services' share of all service exports rose from 12% to 19%, while creative goods' share of all goods exports has remained steady around 3% since 2002. Developing countries primarily export creative goods, while developed countries dominate creative services exports. However, developing countries have significantly increased their share from 10% in 2010 to 20% in 2022.

The most exported creative services in 2022 were software services (41.3%) and research and development (30.7%), followed by advertising, market research, and architecture (15.5%), audiovisual services (7.9%), information services (4%), and cultural, recreational, and heritage services (0.6%).

### **Digital transformation in creative industries**

Digitalization is transforming the creative economy. For example, streaming services expanded their share of the global music market by 10.4% year-on-year, and now account for 67.3% of global music revenue streams. AI is a key driver of this change, enhancing content creation, distribution and consumption. It generates scripts, movies, news articles, music, images, captions, animations and virtual reality content, while improving post-production workflows and analyzing user data.

AI is widely used in newsrooms, with 41% of news teams employing it to create illustrative art, 39% for social media content, and 38% for writing and generating articles. Digital tools lower costs and expand revenue opportunities, making global markets more accessible for individual creators. However, they also bring challenges such as quality control, copyright issues, privacy concerns, and market monopolization. Robust regulatory frameworks are necessary to ensure these technologies benefit everyone and foster a competitive creative economy.

### **Growing market concentration threatens innovation**

Market concentration poses significant challenges to the creative economy. Dominant players can stifle innovation and limit opportunities for smaller firms. For instance, in the USA, the "Big Five" publishers hold about 80% of the book market, while six movie studios account for nearly 90% of box office ticket sales in recent years. In 2021, three companies dominated 59% of the global streaming music subscription market.

Effective policies in some countries have fostered thriving creative sectors by protecting intellectual property and ensuring competitive markets. The report emphasizes the need for a level playing field to promote a diverse and vibrant creative economy.

### **Embracing inclusion and sustainability**

Inclusion and environmental sustainability are becoming a cornerstone of the creative economy. A headline example was Coldplay cutting its recent global tour’s CO2 emissions by 50%, showcasing efforts to reduce the environmental impact of large-scale events.

Governments are integrating inclusion and environmental sustainability into policies targeting the creative economy. Twelve out of 36 countries surveyed have specific initiatives for creative industries, while another twelve promote sustainable business practices, including sustainable design, energy efficiency, circular economy practices, and increasing the participation of women, youth and disadvantaged groups.

## Podcasts

#### [Bright ideas: How creativity can power economies and development](/podcast/bright-ideas-how-creativity-can-power-economies-and-development)

The Weekly Tradecast looks at the economic power and potential of creativity with our special guest, John Howkins, who coined the term "creative economy".

#### [K-popification: Why South Korea’s creative economy is an inspiration for others.](/podcast/k-popification-why-south-koreas-creative-economy-inspiration-others)

This episode of The Weekly Tradecast looks at K-pop and other lessons for success from the creative economy of the Republic of Korea with UNCTAD economist Katalin Bokor.

SUMMARY:
The creative sector accounts for nearly 20% of Washington state's economy, contributing nearly $120 billion annually to the state GDP.

FULL CONTENT:

# Report: Creative economy contributes nearly $120 billion to Washington state GDP

***Commerce study outlines priorities, strategies to grow creative sector which represents nearly 20% of total state economic production***

OLYMPIA, WA – The creative sector accounts for nearly 20% of Washington state’s economy, contributing nearly $120 billion annually to the state GDP, according to a report released today from the Washington State Department of Commerce. The report, produced with the assistance of the Creative Economy Strategic Work Group and lead contractor Western States Arts Federation (WESTAF), provides a strategic pathway for future growth of this key industry sector.

[Read the full Creative Economy Strategy online](https://choosewashingtonstate.com/why-washington/our-key-sectors/creative-economy/strategic-plan/). The study was requested by the 2021-22 State Legislature

“This study confirms that Washington state is a world leader in creative sector jobs,” said Commerce Director Mike Fong. “Innovation is a key driver of our economy, and this sector enriches the arts and cultures of our communities as well as technological advances, from artificial intelligence and quantum computing to new methods for producing clean energy.”

Fong said the results of this study will help guide the future growth of Washington’s creative economy, noting the work group findings align with the state’s commitment to ensuring that economic growth across the state is diverse, equitable, and inclusive.

In its findings, the workgroup reported that the creative sector contributes $119.2 billion to the state’s gross domestic product (GDP), which is 19.6% of Washington’s total economic production.

The study was produced by 34 creative economy stakeholders with input from more than 400 industry representatives. It shows that the creative sector employed 383,001 workers in 2021, which accounted for 9% of total employment. While the majority of the industry is located in urban centers, creative economy jobs can be found in every corner of the state.

In determining a strategy for future growth, the study identified four priorities:

To support these goals, the workgroup identified 11 interrelated policy areas along with initial recommendations:

“The Creative Economy Strategic Plan is important to me, as a Native entrepreneur, because my voice was heard. The thought and detailed actions to implement a plan that is inclusive is something that the world leader in creative-sector jobs, Washington state, is paving the way for all others. This plan has outlined barriers in order to be inclusive, recognizing the imbalance of current structures and redirecting equitable funding and support,” said work group member Mary Big Bull-Lewis, co-owner of Wenatchi Wear and R Digital Design, and chair of Indigenous Roots and Reparations Foundation in Wenatchee.

Creative technology accounted for the most significant number of jobs in the state (59%), which includes careers in the information and communications field. During the study period, the creative technology sector experienced exponential growth (130%), though some growth could be attributed to the shifting needs of the pandemic. Music, theater, dance and visual arts employed 50,474 individuals (13%). Fashion; film, television and radio; and cultural institutions were the three smallest positive-growth contributors to creative economy employment.

[Learn more about all of Washington’s key industry sectors on the Commerce website](https://www.commerce.wa.gov/growing-the-economy/key-sectors/).

enriched_points.expanded_content: The creative economy is a fast-evolving landscape, integrating diverse industries like arts, technology, and media, driving innovation and economic growth globally.
It fuels economic activities through unique talents and collaborative efforts, producing goods and services that enrich cultural and social experiences.
In the U.S., the creative economy contributes approximately $730 billion, representing 4.2% of GDP, with significant growth seen over recent decades, highlighting its economic importance.
